title: Olivier salad
subtitle: Russian potato and vegetable salad with mayonnaise
excerpt: Diced potato, carrot, egg, gherkin, and peas bound in mayonnaise. Russia's most popular salad, served at every festive table, the original version of what the West calls 'Russian salad'.
sourceNotes: Olivier salad was invented in the 1860s by Lucien Olivier, a Belgian chef at the Hermitage restaurant in Moscow, who made an elaborate version with crayfish tails, game, caviar, and capers. The recipe was simplified during the Soviet era into the version familiar today: potato, carrot, egg, gherkin, peas, and mayonnaise. This simpler version became the definitive Soviet festive salad and remains the most-made salad in Russia and Ukraine. The Western 'Russian salad' (insalata russa, salade russe) derives from the same original recipe via different simplifications.

content.text: The key to Olivier salad is the dice: every ingredient cut to the same size, roughly 7 to 8 mm cubes. This is not decorative, uniform dice means every forkful contains all the components. Larger pieces mean some bites are all potato; smaller pieces collapse into a paste. Cut them all the same.

The salad must be chilled before serving. Made and dressed hot, it is just boiled vegetables in mayonnaise. After an hour in the fridge, the mayonnaise has set around the components and the flavours have settled into each other.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: boiled whole in skins, cooled, peeled, and diced

items.name: Carrot
items.prepNote: boiled whole, cooled, peeled, and diced

items.name: Frozen peas
items.prepNote: cooked in boiling water for 2 minutes, drained and cooled

content.text: Ensure all cooked vegetables are completely cold before combining. Mix the diced {{potato}}, {{carrot}}, {{eggs}}, {{gherkins}}, {{peas-frozen}}, and {{spring-onion}} in a large bowl. Add the {{mayonnaise}} and mix gently until all the components are evenly coated. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour before serving, or overnight.

items.fix: Pat gherkins dry before dicing. Ensure all vegetables are fully cooled and drained, any residual heat or water will thin the mayonnaise over time in the fridge
items.cause: Vegetables not completely cooled, or gherkins too wet

items.fix: Add a little extra mayonnaise just before serving and fold in gently
items.symptom: Salad too dry after overnight refrigeration

items.fix: Add a teaspoon of gherkin brine from the jar. This adds both acidity and salt without making the salad taste pickled
items.cause: Not enough seasoning or gherkin acid

content.text: Olivier salad is the centrepiece of the Russian New Year's table, made in quantities sufficient to last several days. It appears at every celebration from birthday parties to wedding receptions, moulded into domes and decorated with egg slices and spring onion, or simply piled into a bowl. Soviet-era New Year's celebrations were planned around two dishes: olivye and shuba (herring under a fur coat). Both still anchor the Russian festive table fifty years on.
